Perplexity's upcoming advertising model looks a lot different than traditional search advertising; it may always look different.



View in browser

Perplexity Search Ads, Inventory-Defined Advertisers, Telegram Follow-Up

Tuesday, August 27, 2024

Listen to this Update in your podcast player

Good morning,

Yesterday\xe2\x80\x99s Sharp Tech was another Mailbag Monday: we covered Disney\xe2\x80\x99s investment in cruise ships, Chick-Fil-A\xe2\x80\x99s streaming service, and Perplexity\xe2\x80\x99s Google Paris ad rip-off/homage .

On to the Update:

Perplexity Search Ads

Speaking of the search upstart, from Digiday :

Perplexity\xe2\x80\x99s plans for competing in the ad space are becoming a bit less puzzling. A new advertiser pitch deck shows how the AI search startup hopes to win over advertiser later this year as it works to set itself apart from competing startups like ChatGPT and giants like Google.

According to a copy of the pitch deck obtained by Digiday, the plan is to integrate ads within users\xe2\x80\x99 queries and answers \xe2\x80\x94 potentially as soon as the fourth quarter of 2024. One page in the deck says buying ads with Perplexity can help brands \xe2\x80\x9creach, educate and spark curiosity of potential customers at high-leverage moments.\xe2\x80\x9d The company is also pitching an educated, affluent and engaged user base. Although the pitch deck didn\xe2\x80\x99t talk about pricing, a source within Perplexity confirmed the goal is to target CPMs \xe2\x80\x9cnorth of $50.\xe2\x80\x9d Total monthly U.S. queries now totaling 230 million \xe2\x80\x94 eight times the total queries it had just a year ago, according to the deck.

One example in the pitch deck shows a Nike ad sponsoring a related question when a user asked about basketball shoes or options for sponsored videos alongside a desktop version. Another shows a sponsored Marriott Bonvoy video ad next to someone asking about \xe2\x80\x9cbest European travel destinations for families.\xe2\x80\x9d Another option is to have \xe2\x80\x9cbranded explanatory text\xe2\x80\x9d that appears above sponsored and organic related questions.

More than a dozen advertising categories are listed as key for the initial launch, according to the pitch deck. Some of those include technology, health and pharma, arts and entertainment and finance food and beverage, sports, science and travel. Others are business, industrial and B2B; internet and telecom; games; automotive; home and garden; beauty and fitness; retail; and shopping.

The key word in this excerpt isn\xe2\x80\x99t a word at all: it\xe2\x80\x99s an acronym. \xe2\x80\x9cCPM\xe2\x80\x9d means \xe2\x80\x9ccost per mille\xe2\x80\x9d, which is the amount an advertiser pays for a thousand impressions; it\xe2\x80\x99s the unit of measurement for your bog-standard display advertising. Perplexity isn\xe2\x80\x99t the only company to use this type of measurement for advertising; consider this press release:

Google Inc., developer of the award-winning Google search engine, announced today at the Jupiter Online Advertising Forum that its targeted online advertising program is delivering highly effective results for advertisers with click-through rates four times higher than the industry average for traditional banner advertising. Google\xe2\x80\x99s advertising program was launched earlier this year with a distinctive approach that helps advertisers target and refine their campaigns to ensure optimal campaign performance.

Google\xe2\x80\x99s advertising program enables advertisers to purchase keywords, keyword phrases or categories to target potential customers. Google uses its proprietary ad serving system to carefully match a user\xe2\x80\x99s search query with a closely related advertisement based on keywords and categories. For example, entering the query \xe2\x80\x9chealth insurance\xe2\x80\x9d into the search box on Google\xe2\x80\x99s home page produces search results and a text-based advertisement that relate to purchasing health insurance online. This enhances the overall search experience for Google users by providing relevant, useful information on the search results page, and benefits advertisers by increasing the likelihood that users will click on a specifically targeted ad.

That press release is, needless to say, pretty old: it was published on August 16, 2000 . It was also obsolete before it even launched; two years earlier GoTo, a competing search engine, had launched the concept of cost-per-click, where advertisers only paid if a consumer actually clicked on their ad. The price for that click was set by an auction. Two years later and Google adopted cost-per-click advertising (and got sued by GoTo, then renamed Overture), and the company was off to the revenue races.

So surely that is next for Perplexity, if not now, then a couple of years down the road?

Inventory-Defined Advertisers

What was so brilliant about the cost-per-click model in the context of search specifically is that it was aligned with the search model itself: it was users that chose both the best ad and the best search result; Google\xe2\x80\x99s job was to facilitate the competition (for search results) and auction (for ads). Moreover, all three markets scaled perfectly, setting Google on the path to being a Super Aggregator .

AI search results make this all a bit more complicated, as I explained in an Update earlier this year :

The Aggregator risks I wrote about yesterday are the exact same risks Google faces from a business model perspective.

  • As long as Google was providing ten blue links, it could serve everyone, no matter their values; after all, the user decided which link to click.
  • By providing ads alongside those ten blue links, Google deputized the end user to choose the winner of the auction it conducted amongst advertisers; those advertisers would pay a premium to win that auction because their potential customer was affirmatively choosing to go to their site, which both meant a higher chance of conversion and the possibility of building a long-term relationship that increased lifetime value.

AI threatens both:

  • Because AI reduces supply to one answer, which, if the subject rests on subjective values, can never satisfy everyone, Google risks upsetting a significant portion of demand, weakening its Aggregator position and potentially decreasing its leverage on costs.
  • Because AI gives an answer instead of links, there is no organic place to put the auction decision; yes, Google could have affiliate links or put some ads alongside the AI answer, but both options are likely less attractive to advertisers and may command less of a premium.

A CPM-based display advertising model is, to be clear, the easiest possible advertising product to start out with: you sell the ads with a sales team and choose the right ad deterministically based on keywords. To that end, \xe2\x80\x9ceasy\xe2\x80\x9d is the most obvious explanation for why Perplexity is reportedly launching the product in this report. It is very much unclear, though, whether Perplexity or any AI search engine will follow the path Google walked so long ago, or, perhaps more accurately, if it will ever monetize as well as cost-per-click search ads do today.

Google is, unsurprisingly, avoiding AI-generated answers for high value commercial searches; for example, I did a search for \xe2\x80\x9cbest life insurance for expats\xe2\x80\x9d:

Perplexity gave what seems, at first glance, to be a more useful answer, with links to guides for choosing the best plan:

Some of those links \xe2\x80\x94 and some of the text \xe2\x80\x94 is very valuable, with direct recommendations and links to plans; surely Perplexity could sell that space? The pitch deck Digiday saw says no:

In April, Chief Business Officer Dmitry Shevelenko told Digiday about the plans for a road map focused on \xe2\x80\x9caccuracy, speed and readability.\xe2\x80\x9d Shevelenko also noted the company couldn\xe2\x80\x99t use the same model as Google and couldn\xe2\x80\x99t pay companies like Apple $10 billion a year for search deals, adding that the company didn\xe2\x80\x99t plan to rank sponsors differently than organic answers.

\xe2\x80\x9cThe weighting will be the same. That\xe2\x80\x99s our version of \xe2\x80\x98Don\xe2\x80\x99t Do Evil,\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d Shevelenko told Digiday earlier this year. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re not going to buy us an answer. Whenever we have advertising, that\xe2\x80\x99s the one thing we don\xe2\x80\x99t do: You can\xe2\x80\x99t pay us to have the answer be different because you\xe2\x80\x99re paying us.\xe2\x80\x9d

That certainly makes sense for Perplexity, which has one other important factor working in their favor: unlike Google, they don\xe2\x80\x99t have any revenue to lose.

Here\xe2\x80\x99s the other thing that is interesting, though, and related: this model actually means that Perplexity isn\xe2\x80\x99t really even competing with Google, at least when it comes to advertisers. There is a sense in digital in which the ad format creates the advertiser; the most famous example is Facebook, which, to take two examples, recovered from the initial COVID slowdown and shook off an advertiser boycott because their advertising base is made up of companies whose existence is predicated on Facebook ads; Google search isn\xe2\x80\x99t maybe quite so extreme, but entire industries are built around search ads, and those advertisers aren\xe2\x80\x99t going to be very interested in this Perplexity product: not only is the monetization model different, but there clearly isn\xe2\x80\x99t any real measurement capabilities in place either.

Instead the obvious customers will be brand advertisers who are both not necessarily looking for a click, but rather to build brand awareness and affinity, and who have infrastructure in place for measuring that (and, helpfully, they have experimental budgets that Perplexity can tap into for now). Moreover, starting with brand advertising is normal for a new consumer product; the more important takeaway is that Perplexity is, from an advertising perspective, not really a search engine, but something more akin to a publication or social network.

That, by extension, might mean that the long-term goal isn\xe2\x80\x99t CPC-type search ads, but cost-per-action ads of the type employed by Meta. That will require a lot of work to build out, including extensive data collection, targeting, and measurement capabilities; at the same time, collecting and collating data in order to present something compelling is actually not that far off from what Perplexity\xe2\x80\x99s core consumer offering already is. Or \xe2\x80\x94 and this might be the true long-run upside \xe2\x80\x94 AI search might end up creating its own advertising base in the long run.

Telegram Follow-Up

Two corrections from yesterday\xe2\x80\x99s Update . First, Telegram only syncs up to 1 million messages, so it\xe2\x80\x99s not a total history. Second, I was wrong to say that other Western messaging services don\xe2\x80\x99t operate in Russia: Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn, and YouTube are banned, but the various messaging services like WhatsApp and Signal are not. That, though, is where it is important to remember that an essential part of Telegram are Channels and large groups, for which WhatsApp and Signal are not competitive for the reasons I laid out yesterday.

As for the reasons behind CEO Pavel Durov\xe2\x80\x99s arrest, the official press release sure seems to suggest that a refusal to moderate is indeed an important part of the warrant (above and beyond delivering information demanded by authorities). In other words, Durov is being held responsible for what some other of the 900 million users of Telegram use the service for; that strikes me as a very dangerous precedent to set, even if some of that usage is objectionable in the extreme.
